section_2_property_description.legal_description_if_no_street_address: Lot 12, Block C, Oak Hollow Subdivision, Phase II, a subdivision in Travis County, Texas, according to the map or plat thereof recorded in Volume 95, Page 214, Plat Records of Travis County, Texas

section_3_reasons_for_protest.other_reason_for_protest: I am also protesting based on the fact that the appraisal does not adequately reflect the actual physical condition of the improvements as of January 1, 2025. The appraisal appears to assume an updated interior and does not account for several deferred maintenance items and original builder-grade finishes. Additionally, the appraisal seems to rely on sales that are significantly larger and more recently renovated than my home, without sufficient downward adjustment for these differences.

section_4_additional_facts.additional_facts_line_1: The 2025 Notice of Appraised Value from Travis Central Appraisal District lists the market value of my property at $589,000. Based on recent comparable sales in my immediate neighborhood and the current condition of my home, I believe a more accurate market value as of January 1, 2025, is approximately $535,000. My home is a 3-bedroom, 2-bath, 1,842 square foot single-story house built in 1996, with an attached 2-car garage and an older composition shingle roof that is nearing the end of its useful life.
section_4_additional_facts.additional_facts_line_2: Several recent sales support my opinion of value. For example, 4805 Oak Hollow Drive (same subdivision, 3/2, 1,820 sq. ft., built 1995) sold on 2024-10-18 for $528,000 and had a fully renovated kitchen and updated bathrooms, which my property does not. Another nearby sale, 4902 Maple Crest Cove (3/2, 1,905 sq. ft., built 1997) sold on 2024-09-02 for $542,000 and included new windows, a replaced roof in 2021, and updated flooring throughout. My home still has original 1996 builder-grade cabinets, countertops, and tile in the bathrooms, as well as original single-pane windows that are drafty and contribute to higher utility bills. The current appraisal appears to assume a condition closer to these updated properties without adequately adjusting downward for my home’s dated interior and deferred maintenance.
section_4_additional_facts.additional_facts_line_3: In addition, there are specific condition issues that negatively affect my property’s value: (1) the roof is 19 years old (last replaced in 2006) and has visible curling shingles and minor granule loss—two roofing contractors have recommended replacement within the next 1–2 years, with quotes in the $13,000–$16,000 range; (2) the HVAC system is from 2011 and is nearing the end of its expected life, with increasingly frequent repairs; (3) there is minor but noticeable cracking in the drywall in the living room and hallway due to normal settling, which a foundation company has said is cosmetic but still detracts from buyer appeal; and (4) the backyard fencing is leaning in several sections and needs partial replacement. These items collectively mean that a typical buyer would either negotiate a lower price or factor in substantial repair and update costs, leading to a true market value below the current appraised amount. For these reasons, I respectfully request that the appraisal review board reduce the appraised market value of my property to $535,000 for the 2025 tax year.
